content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 4, 2016

Law Office Of
Andreas C. Rockas
1121 L Street, Suite 103
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-245

Dear Mr. Rockas:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of your client, the Almond Hullers
& Processors Association regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Are contributions received by the Almond PAC through the Program (described below)
contributions received through an intermediary? If so, what are the Almond PAC's campaign
disclosure requirements under the Act?

2. When should contributions through the Program described below be reported by the
Almond PAC as being "made" and as being "received" by the Almond PAC?

3. Would the answers to Questions 1 or 2 change if the Program allowed the handler to
withhold a reasonable service fee from a grower's contribution before it sent the contribution along
to the Almond PAC?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. Under the Act, contributions by intermediaries must be fully disclosed as made from
the original contributor through the intermediary. Almond PAC is required to publicly disclose
information about the political contributions, the growers as the source of the contributions, and the
handlers as the intermediary.

2. Because the anticipated agreements will designate the handlers as the growers' agents and
Almond PAC will not have control over the contributions until received from the handler, the
grower's monetary contribution is "made" on the date the contribution is mailed, delivered or

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-245
Page No. 2

otherwise transmitted by the handler to the Almond PAC. The contribution is "received" on the
date that the Almond PAC obtains possession or control of the check or other negotiable instrument
by which the contribution is made.

3. No. However, you state that the handler will provide the Almond PAC with the full
contribution amount the handler was authorized to set aside on behalf of that grower. Under the
proposed Program, this would require the handler to impose a fee in addition to the contribution.
Because the handler is an agent of the grower, the fee expense would be charged to the grower.

FACTS

The Almond Hullers & Processors Association ("AHPA") is a 35-year old California-based
nonprofit trade association that represents the interests of its members who are mostly almond
growers, hullers/sellers and handlers. AHPA's primary purpose is to promote the almond industry
as a whole. AHPA has a sponsored political committee, the California Almond Industry Almond
PAC ("Almond PAC"), which advocates on behalf of its members and the industry.

In the almond industry, growers deliver their harvested almonds (hulled and/or shelled) to a
"handler" who then sells, consigns, and transports the almonds into the channels of international
trade. The handler receives payment for the almonds placed in trade, and then pays the grower for
the almonds, according to the purchase agreement and market price. Several handlers wish to begin
a program to facilitate contributions to the Almond PAC from almond growers who are not
members of the AHPA.

The handlers propose a program ("the Program") whereby they solicit and obtain written
agreements from their existing growers authorizing the handler to act as a grower's agent for
making political contributions to the Almond PAC. The grower would agree to allow the handler to
withhold a portion of the money due from the sale of their almonds to make political contributions
to the Almond PAC. The handler will transmit the grower's contribution to the Almond PAC's
agent (treasurer) on a monthly or quarterly basis. The handler would not charge the Almond PAC
for its efforts. The handler may consider charging the grower an additional handling fee for the
transaction. The proposed written agreements would be in the form of a new agreement with a
grower, or a modified version of the handler/grower's existing purchase agreement.

This Program would be structured so that the Almond PAC would not exercise any control
over any contribution facilitated by the handler until the Almond PAC received the contribution.
For reporting purposes, the handler would separately identify and disclose to the Almond PAC each
participating grower as the original source of a contribution, in the full amount the handler was
authorized to set aside on behalf of that grower. The date of the grower's contribution would be the
date the handler sent the contribution to the Almond PAC. The grower's contribution would be
reported by the Almond PAC as being received on the date the Almond PAC's agent received it.

File No. A-15-245
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

1. Are contributions received by the Almond PAC through the Program (described below) contributions received through an intermediary? If so, what are the Almond PAC's campaign disclosure requirements under the Act?

The Act provides that one person might act as an intermediary by collecting a contribution for the benefit of another person. (Section 84302 and Regulation 18432.5.) The source of the resulting contribution to the second person is the original contributor, not the intermediary. Under the Act, contributions by intermediaries must be fully disclosed as made from the original contributor through the intermediary.

Section 84302 states:

"No person shall make a contribution on behalf of another, or while acting as the intermediary or agent of another, without disclosing to the recipient of the contribution both his own full name and street address, occupation, and the name of his employer, if any, or his principal place of business if he is self-employed, and the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of employer, if any, or principal place of business if self-employed, of the other person. The recipient of the contribution shall include in his campaign statement the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any, or the principal place of business if self-employed, of both the intermediary and the contributor."

Regulation 18432.5(a) further provides that a person is an intermediary for a contribution under two circumstances: (1) if the recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be the contributor without the disclosure of the identity of the true source of the contribution or (2) if the person is an intermediary because under Regulation 18419 they are an intermediate unit of a sponsor that forwards contributions.

We have advised that a person will be considered an intermediary if, at the time of the contribution, the original contributor knowingly and unambiguously earmarked the contribution for deposit with the second person. (See, e.g., Gray Advice Letter, No. A-03-068.) Section 85704's prohibition on earmarking states that "[a] person may not make any contribution to a committee on the condition or with the agreement that it will be contributed to any particular candidate unless the contribution is fully disclosed pursuant to Section 84302."

In this matter, handlers would be deemed "intermediaries" acting on behalf of a contributor in delivering contributions to a committee, Almond PAC. The Program will be structured so that the handlers are the agents of the grower and not the Almond PAC. The handlers will only take grower contributions for the Almond PAC. The handlers will actively approach identified non-member growers and solicit their contributions. However, your facts indicate that the handlers are individual businesses not formed for a political purpose; and the handlers will not engage in any

File No. A-15-245
Page No. 4

additional activities related to political contributions or fundraising.² Specifically, you have not
indicated that the handlers will be soliciting funds for particular candidates or measures. The facts
provided suggest that the handlers will simply provide a means for growers who are not members of
AHPA to contribute money to the Almond PAC. Therefore, given the current structure of the
Program, and the limited role of the handlers, the handlers would be acting solely as intermediaries
in receiving contributions.

Under Section 84302 and Regulation 18432.5, Almond PAC must disclose the information
required regarding handlers and contributing growers. Regulation 18432.5 (b) and (c) state the
following disclosure requirements relating to intermediaries:

"(b) Any person who qualifies as an intermediary for the making of a contribution
shall disclose to the recipient of the contribution both his or her own full name and
street address, occupation, and the name of his employer, if any, or his or her
principal place of business if he or she is self-employed, and the full name and street
address, occupation, and the name of employer, if any, or principal place of business
if self-employed, of the contributor.

"(c) The recipient of the contribution shall include in his or her campaign statement
the name of the intermediary and other information disclosed pursuant to
Government Code Section 84302 and subdivision (b) of this section, if the recipient
knows or has reason to know that a contribution is made by an intermediary."

In the event that the handlers' limited role in this Program changes in regards to its
activities, relationship with the growers, or relationship with Almond PAC, the handlers may be
subject to additional reporting requirements under the Act. (See Sections 84100-84511, generally.)

2. When should contributions through the Program described below be reported by the
Almond PAC as being "made" and as being "received" by the Almond PAC?

Regulation 18421.1 states the following regarding disclosure of the making and receipt of
contributions:

"(a) A monetary contribution, including one made through wire transfer,
credit card transaction, debit account transaction or similar electronic payment
option (including one made via the Internet), is "made" on the date that the
contribution is mailed, delivered, or otherwise transmitted to the candidate or
committee. Alternatively, the date of the check or other negotiable instrument by
which the contribution is made may be used in lieu of the date on which the
contribution is mailed, delivered, or otherwise transmitted, provided it is no later
than the date the contribution is mailed, delivered, or otherwise transmitted.

² There is no indication in your facts that employees of the handlers would be spending more than 10% of their
compensated time in any month rendering services for political purposes which could give rise to a reportable
contribution under Regulation 18423.

File No. A-15-245
Page No. 5

"(c) A monetary contribution is "received" on the date that the candidate
or committee, or the agent of the candidate or committee, obtains possession or
control of the check or other negotiable instrument by which the contribution is
made. All contributions received by a person acting as an agent of a candidate or
committee shall be reported to and disclosed by the candidate or committee, or by
the committee's treasurer, no later than the closing date of the next campaign
statement that the committee or candidate is required to file."

Regulation 18421.1(d) and (e) set forth a different standard where the contribution is
collected by means of a payroll deduction or membership, or received by an "agent" of the
committee. Both the situations in 18421.1(d) and (e) reflect the time period in which the committee
is deemed to be "in possession or control" of the contribution.

In this proposal, the written agreements will clearly state that the handlers are acting as
agents for the growers and not for the Almond PAC. Further, it is anticipated that the agreements
will make clear that the Almond PAC would not exercise control over any grower's contribution
until the PAC's treasurer received the contribution from the handler.

Because the anticipated agreements will designate the handlers as the growers' agents and
the Almond PAC will not have control over the contributions until received from the handler, the
grower's monetary contribution is "made" on the date the contribution is mailed, delivered or
otherwise transmitted by the handler to the Almond PAC. The contribution is "received" on the
date that the Almond PAC, obtains possession or control of the check or other negotiable
instrument by which the contribution is made.

3. Would the answers to Questions 1 or 2 above change if the grower allowed the handler to
withhold a reasonable service fee from a grower's contribution before it sent the contribution along
to the Almond PAC?

As stated above, handlers are considering withholding a fee from the growers for the
expense involved in acting as the grower's intermediary. You state that the handlers will disclose to
Almond PAC each participating grower as being the original source of a contribution, and provide
the full amount the handler was previously authorized to set aside on behalf of that grower.
Presumably, then, the fee charged to the grower will be held back from the proceeds otherwise due
to the grower in addition to the authorized contribution amount, rather than from the "contribution."

Such a proposal does not appear to pose any additional issues, so long as the expenses are
between the handler and the grower, and are not reflected in a reduction of the contribution. If the
contribution is reduced by a fee, then the amount of the actual contribution to the Almond PAC
would need to be reflected in the reports of the Almond PAC. Because the handler is an agent of
the grower, the fee expense would be charged to the grower. (See, e.g., Turner Advice Letter No.
A-05-020, pp. 3 and 4, for a discussion of credit card fees as expenditures attributable to a
committee.)

File No. A-15-245
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl
